# Transcrição: 02- 20 - Framing Your Story

- **Fonte:** 02- 20 - Framing Your Story
- **Duração:** 21:38
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right, congratulations. If you're here, you have a thesis statement for your story or you're a cheater. If you're a cheater, go back and get a thesis statement because you can't do this step unless you have a thesis statement in place. We're going to frame out your story now. Framing your story is essentially determining the boundaries of your story, the beginning and the end with some specificity. If you have a thesis statement, you have general area that you're going to begin and a general area that you're going to end, we're going to find some exactitude in those locations. We're going to identify specifically where a story should begin, where it should end, and some strategies for both the beginning and the ending of a story. Let's start with the ending. We always start with the end because we need to know what we're aiming at. We need to know what that final moment of the story is going to be. As we craft it, we can aim all of our details and all of our scenes and transitions, all of those decisions that we're going to be making. We want to have a place to aim them, so we aim them at the end. When you're choosing an ending for a story, a couple things to keep in mind. You want the ending to be as close to that five-second moment as possible because every word you speak after your five-second moment is going to be listened to less by your audience. Once you hit that moment and everyone feels what you feel and you say the thing you've been waiting to say, if you keep talking, you're going to lose your audience. Sometimes that five-second moment can actually be the last thing that happens in the story. My story, it doesn't work out that way because my five-second moment is a sentence that my daughter speaks in a tube over my head. But if I stop the story there, they never get out of the tube. Part of the story has to be, and so they eventually get out of the tube. Charlie reaches his hand across to his sister, she takes his hand in hers, and she pulls him across and they exit the tubes. But that's the end of my story right there. They're going to reach hands across and pull him across the net. In fact, I don't even know if I have to get them out of the tubes. He just has to get over that mesh net section so that I know he's safe. So my five-second moment is pretty close to the actual end of my story, which is great. Those are the easiest ways to end a story. When we're ending a story, we always want to do three things. When we describe that five-second moment and maybe the little bit that happens afterwards, we want to first describe what we see. We want the audience to see it in their mind's eye. And then we want to tell the audience what we feel, what we're feeling in our heart, and then we want to tell our audience what we're thinking. We want to do all three of those things. Now I'm a novelist. In a novel, I would not do all three of those things because

## [03:02 - 06:05]

novels demand subtlety. Novels allow a reader to pause on a page, linger over a sentence, pause and think about what the words mean, but not in a story. In a story, I'm talking, and they have to keep up with me. There is no time for an audience member to pause, to linger over what I've said, to consider the meaning of my sentences because I'm still talking and they need to keep up. So the order I always like to say is I'm going to show it to them, I'm going to tell them how it makes me feel, and then I'm going to tell them how it makes me think. I'm going to get all three of those things so I have absolute clarity at the end of a story that the audience knows what the moment looked like, what it felt to me, and what it caused me to think in my brain, in my mind. So look for those three things. What are you going to say to your audience? How are you going to describe it? How did it make you feel? And what did it make you think? And then end the story as quickly as possible. In fact, one of the things you might want to consider doing at the end of your story is leaving a little bit unsaid. Stories that get wrapped up in perfect bows at the end, they tend to be problematic because they allow the audience to disengage entirely with the story. If everything is perfectly clear and clean and there's no lingering questions at the end of a story, then the audience can walk away from that story and not worry about it anymore. But if you leave a little bit unsaid, if you're able to leave a little bit of that rope frayed at the end instead of neatly tied, then the audience will linger on it longer and longer, and that's good for you. You want your audience thinking about you once you're done speaking. Think about movies that have ended with a question. Movies that have ended with, well, wait a minute, did they end up getting together? Did they get married? Did he make it all the way home? Was that the defeat of the empire or is there more? Those are the movies that linger with us the most. Think about the books you've read, the ones where you wished there was one more chapter. You wished that there was a sequel. Those are the books that linger with us the most. The ones that wrap everything up into a neat bow, we can push them aside and forget them. So when you're ending your story, think about what you might leave out. Maybe I'll leave Charlie and Clara up in that tube on the other side of the mesh together. I'll leave them right there. It's not much. The audience is going to assume they eventually get out of the tube, but at least by leaving them up there, that's the last image I give the audience is that image of two kids in a tube. I think it's going to cause the audience to linger over the story a little bit more. Remember two kids in a tube instead of two kids on the ground. I think it's a little more memorable. See if you can make a choice like that at the end of the story. Once you've made all those choices,

## [06:05 - 09:07]

you have an end to your story. It's time to figure out where the beginning is. That'll complete the frame. To find the beginning, we just ask ourselves what's the opposite of the end. So if at the end of the story, I feel like my kids are going to be okay. They're not going to need me. They can find their way through this life if I am not around. The beginning of the story has to be the opposite of that. My kids are dependent upon me. They need me. They can't get through life without me. That's the place I need to begin my story. That's the place emotionally and mentally I need to begin, but now I need to choose the location. Where am I going to begin my story? We're going to follow a few rules to choose that right spot. The first and most important rule is start as close to the end as possible. And this is not my rule. This is Kurt Vonnegut's rule for writing short stories that we can apply to the stories that we speak aloud. Start as close to the end as possible. It has enormous benefits for you. The first being it's going to shorten your story. And the shortest version of every story is almost the best version of every story. So by starting as close to the end as possible, we're going to chop off unnecessary content from your story. It's also going to push everything close together. It's going to heighten the excitement and the drama and the suspense of your story by jamming everything into the smallest amount of time possible. In the hands of someone who doesn't understand these principles, my story might begin with I pull into McDonald's. I'm bringing my kids to breakfast and they're going to play at the play place. I hear those beginnings all the time. That's a terrible beginning. Why am I in my car when my car has nothing to do with my story? Why am I outside the McDonald's when outside the McDonald's has nothing to do with my story? Why am I not in the play place at the beginning of my story? Because that is where my story takes place. So I'm going to leave all of that out. I'm going to start my story for sure in the play place. Now let me see if I can get even closer to the end. Do I start with the kids eating pancakes and Charlie finishing his first and going up into the tubes? No. Why not have Charlie already in the tubes? Why not have him in the play place and Clary eating pancakes? In fact, why not have Charlie already in that tube over my head having him already crossed that net one time all the way on the end and coming back? Why not have Charlie just about to get in trouble? Just about to reach that net a second time and get stuck. That is as close to the end as possible as I can get and that's where I'm going to start. I'm going to start my story seconds before Charlie gets in trouble. That's a great place to start. Can you feel how immediately there's going to be drama and suspense and a problem at the beginning of my story?

## [09:08 - 12:14]

Rather than pulling into the parking lot where there's no problems and no drama, rather than ordering our pancakes, which is going to be boring and pedestrian, rather than having kids eat pancakes at a table, which is definitely not going to entertain anybody, why not have a four-year-old boy in a tube and he's already in trouble at the beginning of the story? You need to do the same thing for your story. You need to move your story as close to the end as possible. It is the most frequent change I make when I'm working with storytellers, is I'm constantly moving the beginning of their story closer to the end, chopping off content that is not important, not relevant, not entertaining, not suspenseful, not dramatic, lacking all stakes. We're going to move our story as close to the end as possible. The next rule is perhaps the easiest rule you can begin applying to your life from here for the rest of your life. In every story you tell for the rest of your life, including the one you're crafting now, begin with two things, two elements, location and action. Every time. Location, where are you? In my story, it's simple. I'm in a play place, which everyone can sort of imagine, which is the power of location. Location is amazing because almost every location that you can offer to an audience is automatically imbued with a thousand adjectives. When I say my kids are playing in a McDonald's play place, I have to say nothing else for you to be able to envision that space. I'm going to have to be a little specific about that mesh net section. I'm going to have to make sure you can see that clearly. I'm going to have to be clear about the size of the tubes and how I really can't access this McDonald's play place that it is too small for me. But other than that, I don't have to tell you that the tubes are yellow and red and blue. You knew that already. I don't have to tell you that the floor is made of some questionably cushiony material because you knew that. I bet you knew that there was a ball pit in my McDonald's play place. I bet you knew that there were little holes in the tubes that the kids could peek out of and wave to you. I bet you could see the tables where kids would sit and eat their food. You probably could smell the McDonald's play place if I ask you to. That is the magic of location. You say one word and people can oftentimes see it with perfect clarity. Let's play a game. I'm going to give you a sentence. I dropped the bowl of blueberries on the kitchen floor. I dropped the bowl of blueberries on the kitchen floor. I forced you to see something in your mind's eye. I know you saw it. I know you saw the kitchen. You can tell me right now if the kitchen floor is wooden, linoleum, or tile. You know it. You saw it. And if the material that comprises the

## [12:14 - 15:19]

kitchen floor is not relevant to my story, I'm going to let your imagination take over for me. I'm going to let you imagine the floor because the power of your imagination will always be more powerful than any assemblage of words that I can offer. So what did you imagine? You probably imagined your own kitchen floor or the kitchen floor in your parents' home or the kitchen floor in a television show that you watch often. Whatever it was, you saw it with great clarity. You can tell me if the appliances in this kitchen are laminate or stainless steel. You can tell me, is this a kitchen where you can see the trash can? Or is this a kitchen with weirdos who put their trash can under their sink as if it doesn't exist? If I asked you to look inside the fridge, you could actually tell me what's inside the fridge of this kitchen that I have created just by saying the word kitchen. And that bowl of blueberries? Was it wooden, glass, or plastic? You know. I don't know what you're thinking, but I don't care because if the material that the bowl is made of is not relevant to my story, once again, I'm going to allow your imagination to choose for me because you will see it with greater clarity. Those blueberries, were they the kinds you pick in summer when they're big and ripe and the most bursting? Or were they sad winter blueberries, small and shriveled? You knew. And again, unless it's relevant to my story, I let you choose. Location allows the audience to take all of the things they know about the location that you have stated and instantly apply it to that location. People ask me all the time, how do you make your stories so clear in my mind? How do you do that? And I always tell them, I despise adjectives and I avoid them whenever possible. Instead, what I rely on is nouns, clear nouns, and I allow the audience to envision them on their own. And unless the specificity of that noun is relevant to my story, I never care what they choose. As a storyteller, I'm not trying to recreate my life with precision for my audience because I don't care about that. I care about feelings, I care about action, I care about dialogue, I care about mental thought. But if they don't envision my kitchen in exactly the same way that my kitchen is laid out, that doesn't matter to me. If this play place in my story is a little different than the one that you're envisioning, I don't care. As long as the story holds together, as long as the particularities of the location are irrelevant to the story, I leave it to you to imagine it fully. That is the power of location, and that is why we always open stories with location. I am somewhere. Where are you in your story? For

## [15:19 - 18:21]

me, I'm in a play place in a McDonald's, which I know you can see. The second one is action. We begin with action because audiences want us to start our story. They don't want to hear exposition, they don't want to learn things before the story begins. They want the story to get started just like it does in a movie. They want the story to take off. So in my story, someone needs to be doing something. It's probably going to be Charlie is crawling through the tube, heading back towards the tower. It's probably going to be Charlie moving because I want the audience is focused there, but whatever it is, someone needs to be moving. Almost always, it's the protagonist, you, in your own story, who is moving, but not always. In this case, probably not. But we start with movement because it engages the audience immediately. If we need to teach the audience something, if they need to understand who Charlie is and where we are and anything else that might be important to the story, we can teach that once the story has begun. My favorite example of this is Apollo 13. If you've ever seen that movie, the Tom Hanks movie about the failed lunar mission, in order to understand that film, you need to understand 1960s space travel. But the movie does not begin with Tom Hanks on a black screen saying, good evening, ladies and gentlemen, before this movie begins, let me teach you a little bit about 1960 space travel so that you will understand the movie better. That's not how it begins because no one ever wants to hear a story like that. Yet people start their stories like that all the time. They start with exposition. I've heard stories start with, let me tell you something about my mother. It turns out no one has ever in the history of the world wanted to hear the second sentence of that story. No one cares about your mother at the beginning of a story unless something is happening that causes us to want to know something about your mother. Now, if I began my story with, I'm climbing out of my bedroom window. I'm hanging out hoping to land on the hedge. I'm running away from home. I'm running away from home because of my mother. Let me tell you something about my mother. Now you want to know because I began my story with location, hanging out of a window over a hedge and action. I'm running away from home. When we start with action, we tell the audience, buckle up. We're already moving forward. Stay with us. This story is already going somewhere. So don't start with exposition. Don't start with teaching. Don't tell the audience things you think they need to know at the beginning of the story. No, tell them after the story has begun. If you begin every story for the rest of your life with simply location and action, you're going to grab audience's attention immediately. I have taught this strategy to 10 year old children and they use it all the time and it has made them exponentially

## [18:21 - 21:25]

better storytellers. It's an easy habit to get into and you should use it every single time you begin a story. It is a perfect beginning strategy to ensure that your story is going to grab the attention of the audience. So that's the frame of your story. We begin at the end with our five second moment. We ask ourselves how far after the five second moment does the story need to continue? What are we going to show? How are we going to feel? And what are we going to think? We ask ourselves what's the opposite of that and that's the beginning of our story, the front of our frame. And there we're going to start as close to the end as possible. We're going to avoid exposition. We're going to start with location and action. If we just do that, create an effective frame for the story. A frame where the beginning and the end of the story are in conversation with each other and in the best of cases in opposition to each other, we're better than most of the people telling stories today because most people begin their stories wherever it chronologically makes sense, meaning I pulled into the McDonald's restaurant. And most people don't actually end stories. Most stories told in the world today end because the phone rings, dinner is served, someone is interrupted, a bell rings. People rarely begin speaking with an idea of where they're going to end. Storytellers are people who think before they speak. Even if the thought is simply where am I going with this story? Where do I want to end? If that's all you can think of before you begin speaking, you're already better than almost everyone telling stories today. Remember, storytelling is about decision making. It has nothing to do with your vocabulary, has nothing to do with your experience with stories in the past or your writing ability. It's make a good decision, which is to say, make a decision because most people speaking today don't think before they speak, which is to say, they don't make any decisions. They just allow the words to pour forth from their mouth with no thought whatsoever. Storytellers think before they speak. So create the frame for your story. Start at the end, find the beginning, use the strategies I've offered to you and lock them into place. Once you have them in place, use the anatomy of a story worksheet. Identify on that worksheet, where is your story going to begin? Where is the story going to end? Fill in those spaces on the worksheet. That will become the frame in which we will work. We won't have to worry about anything that happens before the story takes place, and we won't have to worry about anything that happens after the story takes place.

## [21:25 - 21:38]

Everything is going to fit within the frame of the story that you will create today. When you're ready, go on to the next video. Good luck.
